steps.title: Find angle CAD using isosceles triangle ACD
steps.content:
Triangle ACD has AC = CD, making it isosceles. The angle ACD = 108° (given). In an isosceles triangle, the base angles are equal, so:
$$\angle CAD = \angle CDA = \frac{180^{\circ} - 108^{\circ}}{2} = \frac{72^{\circ}}{2} = 36^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Find angle ACB using supplementary angles on line DB
steps.content:
Since C lies on line segment DB, angles ACD and ACB form a linear pair along that line—they are supplementary:
$$\angle ACB = 180^{\circ} - \angle ACD = 180^{\circ} - 108^{\circ} = 72^{\circ}$$
Now we know angle ACB = 72° in triangle ABC.

steps.title: Use triangle angle sums to find x
steps.content:
In triangle ABC:
$$\angle ABC + \angle BAC + 72^{\circ} = 180^{\circ}$$
$$\angle ABC + \angle BAC = 108^{\circ}$$
Since E lies on line segment DA, angle BAE equals angle BAD. We can write:
$$\angle BAD = \angle BAC + \angle CAD = \angle BAC + 36^{\circ}$$
Next, observe that angle ABE relates to angle ABC through the given angle EBC = 29°. From point B, ray BE lies between rays BA and BC, so:
$$\angle ABE = \angle ABC - \angle EBC = \angle ABC - 29^{\circ}$$
In triangle ABE, the angles sum to 180°:
$$(\angle ABC - 29^{\circ}) + (\angle BAC + 36^{\circ}) + x^{\circ} = 180^{\circ}$$
Rearranging:
$$\angle ABC + \angle BAC + x^{\circ} - 29^{\circ} + 36^{\circ} = 180^{\circ}$$
$$\angle ABC + \angle BAC + x^{\circ} + 7^{\circ} = 180^{\circ}$$
$$108^{\circ} + x^{\circ} + 7^{\circ} = 180^{\circ}$$
$$x^{\circ} = 65^{\circ}$$
The answer is 65.
